policy_statement: NEOM will build one of the world’s largest green hydrogen facilities powered by over 4 GW of renewable energy; once onstream by 2025 it is expected to produce 650 tons/day of green hydrogen and 1.2 million tons/year of green ammonia.
mechanism_description: NEOM will build a renewable-powered green hydrogen and ammonia facility that produces hydrogen via electrolysis, displacing fossil-derived hydrogen/ammonia and lowering associated greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Energy supply decarbonization (renewables → green H₂) → fossil H₂/NH₃ displacement → ↓ lifecycle GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Build and operate renewable-powered electrolysis plus downstream ammonia synthesis and associated export/use infrastructure, increasing availability of “green” hydrogen/ammonia.  
2) Mediating physical/industrial process: Renewable electricity splits water to produce hydrogen; using this hydrogen for ammonia synthesis or as a fuel/industrial input avoids the steam methane reforming/coal gasification pathways and their process emissions (and can reduce upstream fossil fuel extraction leakage).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions (primarily CO₂, also potentially CH₄ upstream) per unit of hydrogen/ammonia or energy service delivered, contingent on low-carbon electricity supply and actual displacement of fossil alternatives.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoiding CO₂ (and some methane-related) emissions by displacing fossil-based hydrogen/ammonia production with renewable-powered electrolysis.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Producing hydrogen with renewable electricity and using it to substitute fossil-derived hydrogen/ammonia reduces lifecycle GHG emissions—this is a direct emissions mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there can be resource-efficiency co-benefits (e.g., cleaner industrial inputs) and possible adaptation relevance (energy security), the largest climate effect across policies using this mechanism is emissions reduction via fuel/feedstock substitution.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the electricity is not additional/clean, if hydrogen is used additively rather than displacing fossil production, or if significant upstream emissions occur (e.g., fossil-powered electrolysis), mitigation benefits can be reduced; nevertheless the mechanism as defined is explicitly “renewable-powered green hydrogen” intended for displacement.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose core mechanism is renewable-powered green hydrogen production intended to displace fossil-derived hydrogen/ammonia has its primary climate effect through GHG mitigation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy primarily targets industrial feedstocks, power storage, heavy transport fuels, or export markets requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level classification.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Key edge cases: (a) grid-connected electrolysis without additional renewables can increase emissions; (b) weak displacement (hydrogen used for new demand) reduces net mitigation; (c) leakage/land-use impacts from renewable buildout and water sourcing/desalination energy can affect lifecycle emissions; (d) if hydrogen is produced from fossil with CCS (“blue hydrogen”), the mechanism differs and classification may still be mitigation but via a different pathway.
key_indicators: “will be building one of the world's largest green hydrogen facilities”; “powered by over four gigawatts of renewable energy from solar and wind”; “produce 650 tons per day of green hydrogen by electrolysis”; “1.2 million tons per year of green ammonia”; “Once onstream by 2025”
co_benefits: Energy security/industrial diversification; potential air-quality improvements from reduced fossil combustion; job creation/technology development
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a target-setting commitment (by 2025 production volumes), but the dominant instrument expressed is physical project delivery (facility + renewables), i.e., infrastructure investment. No explicit subsidy/regulatory requirement is mentioned in the text.
